coredata.dc:description: This study investigates thermal gradients and chemical degradation in high-nickel LiNi₀.₈Mn₀.₁Co₀.₁O₂ (NMC811) lithium-ion batteries under high-rate discharge conditions (1C–5 C) using a Pseudo-Two-Dimensional (P2D) electrochemical-thermal model. The model, implemented in COMSOL Multiphysics, quantifies temperature distributions, crystallographic strain, and electrolyte decomposition effects on battery performance. At 5 C, the maximum temperature reaches 56.8 °C at the electrode-electrolyte interface, with a coefficient of variation (CV) of 20.2 %, driven by a 12 % increase in heat generation from strain-induced phase transitions and a 10 % rise in internal resistance from hydrofluoric acid (HF) formation. Sensitivity analyses reveal that ±20 % variations in electrolyte decomposition rate and phase transition temperature alter thermal gradients by 0.9–1.3 °C, informing thermal management strategies. Cobalt-rich coatings reduce heat generation by 8–12 %, while vinylene carbonate additives decrease HF formation by 15–20 %, though with trade-offs in energy density and ionic conductivity. Model validation against experimental data yields a root mean square error (RMSE) of 0.08 at 0.15 C. These findings highlight the synergistic impact of structural and chemical degradation on thermal performance, offering insights for optimizing lithium-ion battery safety and longevity in electric vehicle applications through advanced thermal management and material design.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigates thermal gradients and chemical degradation in high-nickel LiNi₀.₈Mn₀.₁Co₀.₁O₂ (NMC811) lithium-ion batteries under high-rate discharge conditions (1C–5 C) using a Pseudo-Two-Dimensional (P2D) electrochemical-thermal model. The model, implemented in COMSOL Multiphysics, quantifies temperature distributions, crystallographic strain, and electrolyte decomposition effects on battery performance. At 5 C, the maximum temperature reaches 56.8 °C at the electrode-electrolyte interface, with a coefficient of variation (CV) of 20.2 %, driven by a 12 % increase in heat generation from strain-induced phase transitions and a 10 % rise in internal resistance from hydrofluoric acid (HF) formation. Sensitivity analyses reveal that ±20 % variations in electrolyte decomposition rate and phase transition temperature alter thermal gradients by 0.9–1.3 °C, informing thermal management strategies. Cobalt-rich coatings reduce heat generation by 8–12 %, while vinylene carbonate additives decrease HF formation by 15–20 %, though with trade-offs in energy density and ionic conductivity. Model validation against experimental data yields a root mean square error (RMSE) of 0.08 at 0.15 C. These findings highlight the synergistic impact of structural and chemical degradation on thermal performance, offering insights for optimizing lithium-ion battery safety and longevity in electric vehicle applications through advanced thermal management and material design.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries with NMC cathodes are pivotal in advancing energy storage technologies for electric vehicles (EVs), renewable energy systems, and portable electronics due to their superior energy density of up to 250 Wh/kg [1]. These batteries offer an extended cycle life of over 1000 cycles with 80 % capacity retention and low self-discharge rates of less than 2 % per month [2]. However, elevated operating temperatures above 50°C accelerate degradation mechanisms, such as solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer growth, cathode lattice degradation, and electrolyte decomposition, which compromise safety, reduce service life, and increase the risk of thermal runaway [1,3,4].

Recent studies emphasize the critical importance of thermal management in addressing challenges associated with NMC-based lithium-ion batteries, particularly the effects of high-rate cycling on cathode phase transitions and electrolyte byproduct formation [5–7]. High-nickel NMC cathodes exhibit accelerated oxygen release at temperatures exceeding 60 °C, resulting in a 20 % capacity fade after 200 cycles at a 4 C discharge rate [8]. Similarly, LiPF6-based electrolytes generate resistive hydrofluoric acid (HF) byproducts under elevated temperatures, leading to an increase in internal resistance by up to 15 % [9]. Nanostructured NMC cathodes with optimized particle morphologies have been shown to mitigate thermal stress, thereby enhancing cycle stability at high C-rates [10]. These findings underscore the necessity for integrated chemical and thermal management strategies to improve the reliability and safety of NMC-based LIBs in high-demand applications.

The thermal performance of LIBs is governed by heat generation from ohmic losses (I2R), activation overpotentials, and entropic contributions, which scale with discharge rate [11]. In NMC cathodes, the high nickel content enhances energy density but compromises thermal stability due to weaker Ni-O bonds, which can lead to oxygen release and exothermic reactions at elevated temperatures [12]. Recent studies have highlighted that temperatures above 50°C can trigger electrolyte decomposition, particularly for LiPF₆-based electrolytes in ethylene carbonate (EC) and dimethyl carbonate (DMC), forming resistive byproducts such as HF that further exacerbate heat generation [13,14]. Moreover, rapid lithium-ion intercalation/deintercalation at high C-rates induces crystallographic strain and phase transitions (e.g., layered-to-spinel transformations) in the NMC lattice, amplifying localized heating and increasing the risk of capacity fade and thermal runaway [15]. For instance, High-nickel NMC811 cathodes exhibit accelerated phase transitions above 55 °C, resulting in a 15 % capacity loss after 100 cycles at 3 C [16]. Incorporating cobalt-rich surface coatings on NMC particles has been shown to mitigate oxygen release, reducing thermal runaway risks at 5 C [17]. Doping NMC cathodes with titanium stabilizes the lattice structure, lowering phase transition rates by 10 °C during high-rate discharges [18]. Electrolyte additives, such as vinylene carbonate (VC), suppress HF formation in LiPF6-based systems, enhancing thermal stability at 60 °C [19]. Nanostructured NMC cathodes with controlled particle sizes have been demonstrated to mitigate crystallographic strain, reducing heat generation by 12 % at 4 C [20]. These chemical and structural factors highlight the importance of precise thermal management strategies to ensure battery reliability in electric vehicles (EVs), where discharge rates up to 5 C are common during acceleration or fast-charging scenarios [21].

Advancements in computational modeling, including the Pseudo-Two-Dimensional (P2D) electrochemical model integrated with thermal simulations, have enabled comprehensive analysis of thermal behavior in lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). Recent studies emphasize the necessity of incorporating temperature-dependent electrochemical parameters and multi-layered thermal management systems to mitigate thermal gradients during high C-rate operations [22–24]. Active cooling systems have been demonstrated to maintain NMC battery temperatures below 45 °C during 4 C discharge cycles, significantly reducing the formation of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer [25]. Optimization of electrolyte compositions with thermally stable salts, such as lithium bis(fluorosulfonyl)imide (LiFSI), has been shown to enhance chemical stability at elevated temperatures [26]. A three-dimensional electrochemical-thermal model has been developed to predict thermal gradients in LIBs with a 5 % error margin relative to experimental measurements at 5 C [27]. Furthermore, a hybrid thermal management system integrating phase-change materials and liquid cooling has been proposed, achieving a 20 % reduction in maximum temperatures during 4 C discharge cycles [28]. These findings underscore the critical synergy between chemical design, advanced computational modeling, and thermal engineering in optimizing the performance and safety of NMC-based LIBs for high-demand applications.

Despite these advancements, gaps remain in understanding the thermal response of NMC batteries under high-rate conditions, particularly regarding the spatial distribution of temperatures (minimum, average, and maximum) and their correlation with chemical degradation mechanisms. This study addresses these gaps by analyzing temperature profiles generated using the Pseudo-Two-Dimensional (P2D) model for the NMC battery, focusing on the external surface (minimum), volume-weighted average, and electrode-electrolyte interface (maximum) temperatures. The objectives are to: (1) quantify the thermal response at C-rates from 1 C to 5 C, (2) elucidate the chemical and crystallographic factors driving heat generation, and (3) provide insights for thermal management in EV applications. By integrating validated electrochemical models with recent chemical insights, this work contributes to the development of safer and more efficient NMC-based LIBs for next-generation energy storage systems.

This study investigates the thermal and electrochemical performance of a pouch-type lithium-ion battery with a high-nickel LiNi₀.₈Mn₀.₁Co₀.₁O₂ (NMC811) cathode, manufactured by LG Chem (Model LG60AH-3.6 V/60Ah). Fig. 1 represents the structure of a pouch-type lithium-ion cell. Table 1 summarizes the key specifications, including electrochemical properties, physical dimensions, and material compositions, used in the Pseudo-Two-Dimensional (P2D) model to ensure accurate simulation of thermal gradients and chemical degradation under high-rate discharge conditions (1C–5 C). These parameters, sourced from [29], provide a robust foundation for the electrochemical and thermal analyses.

The specific properties of the cell materials, including density (ρ = 2500 kg/m³), specific heat capacity (Cp = 1100 J/kg.K), and thermal conductivity (k = 30 W/m.K for electrodes, 0.6 W/m.K for electrolyte), were adopted from literature values for NMC811 cathodes, graphite anodes, and 1 M LiPF6 in EC:DMC electrolyte [31]. Electrochemical properties, such as solid-phase diffusion coefficients (Ds = 1 × 10⁻¹⁴ m²/s for NMC, 3.9 × 10⁻¹⁴ m²/s for graphite), electrolyte diffusion coefficient (De = 2 × 10⁻¹⁰ m²/s), and reaction rate constants (k = 5 × 10⁻¹¹ m².⁵/mol1.5.s for NMC, 2 × 10⁻¹¹ m².⁵/mol¹.⁵.s for graphite), were sourced from [32–34]. These parameters were validated through sensitivity analysis in Section 2.4.2, where variations of ±20 % in Ds and k resulted in minimal changes in maximum temperature (0.6–1.1 °C) and voltage deviation (<0.5 %), confirming their accuracy against experimental data (RMSE = 0.08 at 0.15 C) [29].

The NMC811 pouch cell’s layered structure, comprising the cathode (NMC811), anode (graphite), separator, and electrolyte (1 M LiPF6 in EC:DMC), exhibits anisotropic behavior due to the distinct material properties of each layer. To account for this, effective thermal conductivity (k), density (ρ), and specific heat capacity (Cp) were estimated using volume-weighted averaging based on the volume fractions of each component. The effective thermal conductivity was calculated using volume fractions of the battery components as [35]:

The use of volume fractions in Eqs. (1), (2), and (3) ensures accurate representation of the composite nature of the battery’s layered structure, consistent with established methods. With Cp,cathode = 900 J/kg.K, Cp,anode = 700 J/kg.K, Cp,separator = 2000 J/kg.K, and Cp,electrolyte = 1500 J/kg.K, yielding Cp,eff = 1100 J/kg.K [31]. These effective properties were incorporated into the P2D model’s thermal equations to accurately simulate heat transfer, accounting for the anisotropic nature of the battery. The electrochemical properties, such as solid-phase diffusion coefficients (Ds = 1 × 10⁻¹⁴ m²/s for NMC, 3.9 × 10⁻¹⁴ m²/s for graphite), electrolyte diffusion coefficient (De = 2 × 10⁻¹⁰ m²/s), and reaction rate constants (k = 5 ×10⁻¹¹ m².⁵/mol¹.⁵·s for NMC, 2 × 10⁻¹¹ m².⁵/mol¹.⁵·s for graphite), were sourced from [32–34]. These parameters were validated through sensitivity analysis in Section 2.4.2, where variations of ±20 % in Ds and k resulted in minimal changes in maximum temperature [32–34]. These parameters were validated through sensitivity analysis in Section 2.4.2, where variations of ±20 % in Ds and k resulted in minimal changes in maximum temperature (0.6–1.1 °C) and voltage deviation (<0.5 %), confirming their accuracy against experimental data (RMSE = 0.08 at 0.15 C) [29].

Electrode balancing was performed to optimize the capacity ratio between the anode and cathode, ensuring efficient lithium-ion utilization and minimizing overpotential losses. The NMC811 cathode and graphite anode were balanced based on their specific capacities and active material loadings, as provided by the manufacturer. The cathode’s maximum lithium concentration (cs,max= 49,000 mol/m³) and anode’s (cs,max= 31,000 mol/m³) were used to calculate the active material volume fractions, with the anode-to-cathode capacity ratio set to approximately 1.1:1 to prevent lithium plating at high C-rates. This ratio was incorporated into the P2D model by adjusting the electrode thicknesses and active surface areas in the governing equations, ensuring realistic electrochemical performance under discharge conditions from 1 C to 5 C.

The electrochemical model for the NMC battery was developed using the P2D framework, which provides a comprehensive representation of the electrochemical processes within the battery while maintaining computational efficiency suitable for thermal simulations in COMSOL Multiphysics. The P2D model, an extension of the Newman model, captures the spatial variations of lithium-ion concentration and potential in the solid (electrode) and liquid (electrolyte) phases, as well as the interactions at the electrode-electrolyte interface [37]. This approach is particularly suitable for simulating lithium-ion batteries under varying discharge rates, as it accounts for diffusion and migration processes across the battery’s thickness, making it ideal for electric vehicle applications [38,39].

The P2D model integrates a set of coupled partial differential equations (PDEs) to describe the electrochemical dynamics of the NMC battery. These equations account for charge and mass transport, electrochemical kinetics, and conservation laws in the electrodes and electrolyte. The model assumes a one-dimensional macroscopic representation along the battery thickness (x-direction) coupled with a pseudo-dimension for particle-scale diffusion in the solid phase, hence the term "pseudo-two-dimensional." The governing equations are derived from first principles and are consistent with established electrochemical modeling frameworks [40,41]. Below, the key equations are presented with boundary conditions tailored to the NMC battery specifications.

The Faraday constant (F) is essential in Eq. This formulation is consistent with standard electrochemical kinetics for lithium-ion batteries, accurately modeling charge transfer at the electrode-electrolyte interface [34,40].

The thermal model simulates heat generation and dissipation in the NMC811 pouch-type lithium-ion battery under high-rate discharge conditions (1 C to 5 C), capturing the spatial and temporal temperature distribution critical for electric vehicle (EV) applications. The model accounts for multiple heat sources, including ohmic losses, activation overpotentials, concentration overpotentials, and entropic heat, which collectively influence thermal gradients and battery performance. Heat dissipation is modeled at the external surfaces via natural convection. The governing energy balance equation for the temperature distribution T (K) is:
(21)

Where ρ (kg/m³) is the density, Cp (J/kg·K) is the specific heat capacity, k (W/m.K) is the thermal conductivity, t (s) is time, and Q (W/m³) is the total heat source term. The parameter values are: ρ = 2500 kg/m³ (average for electrodes and electrolyte), Cp = 1100 J/kg.K, k = 30 W/m.K for electrodes and 0.6 W/m.K for the electrolyte (1 M LiPF6 in EC:DMC) [45]. The heat source term Q comprises contributions from various losses, detailed below.

Heat from activation overpotentials is generated due to charge transfer resistance at the electrode-electrolyte interface, modeled using the Butler-Volmer equation:

This term reflects energy losses during electrochemical reactions, particularly pronounced at high C-rates.

Reversible entropic heat arises from entropy changes during lithium intercalation/deintercalation, varying with state of charge (SOC):

Where ∂U/∂T (V/K) is the entropic coefficient, typically on the order of 0.1 mV/K for NMC811 cathodes, derived from experimental measurements [49]. This term can be positive or negative depending on the SOC and reaction direction.

Heat dissipation at the battery’s external surfaces is modeled using Newton’s law of cooling for natural convection:
(27)

Where h = 7 W/m².K is the convective heat transfer coefficient, Tamb = 298.15 K (25°C) is the ambient temperature, and n is the normal vector to the surface [50]. This boundary condition simulates heat transfer to the ambient environment, critical for pouch-type cells in EV battery packs.

The initial temperature is uniform across the battery:
(28) T(x, 0) = 298.15 K

The initial state of charge (SOC) is 100 %, corresponding to a uniform lithium concentration in the solid phase (cs = 49,000 mol/m³ for NMC cathode, 31,000 mol/m³ for graphite anode) and electrolyte (ce = 1000 mol/m³). These conditions align with experimental setups for fully charged batteries, ensuring accurate simulation of thermal behavior under discharge.

This comprehensive thermal model, integrated with the P2D electrochemical framework in COMSOL Multiphysics, provides detailed insights into heat generation and dissipation mechanisms. By quantifying each heat source (ohmic, activation, concentration, and entropic), the model elucidates the factors driving thermal gradients, informing the design of effective thermal management strategies for NMC811 batteries in high-rate EV applications.

The P2D model was implemented in COMSOL Multiphysics. The battery domain was discretized along the x-direction (macroscale) with 100 elements for the electrodes and separator, and the particle-scale diffusion was resolved using 20 radial elements per particle. The time-dependent solver used a backward differentiation formula (BDF) with adaptive time-stepping to ensure numerical stability. The model was initialized with a uniform lithium concentration in the electrolyte (ce= 1000 mol/m3) and solid phase (based on 100 % SOC). The electrochemical parameters, such as internal resistance (R0= 13 mΩ), parallel branch resistance (R1= 5.5 mΩ), and capacitance (C1= 40 kF), were directly adopted from the equivalent circuit model parameters provided in [29]. The battery was assumed to be in a controlled environment with an ambient temperature of 25 °C and natural convection cooling (heat transfer coefficient of 7 W/m².K) applied to the external surfaces [51]. Initial conditions set the battery temperature uniformly at 25 °C, and the SOC was initialized at 100 % for all discharge simulations. Discharge simulations were performed at constant currents corresponding to 0.5 C, 1 C, 2 C, 3 C, 4 C, and 5 C, until the cell voltage reached the discharge cut-off of 2.75 V. These rates were selected to cover a range of operational conditions relevant to electric vehicle applications, including standard and high-power scenarios.

The P2D model’s computational complexity arises from solving coupled partial differential equations across 200 x-direction elements and 40 radial elements per particle, resulting in approximately 10,000 degrees of freedom. Using COMSOL Multiphysics on a standard workstation, a 5 C discharge simulation requires a BDF solver. This complexity limits real-time application in embedded battery management systems (BMS), which require millisecond-scale computations. For BMS integration, reduced-order models, such as single-particle models with simplified thermal dynamics, could lower computational demand while retaining key electrochemical and thermal predictions. These simplifications, validated against the full P2D model, reduce solve time to seconds, making them feasible for real-time monitoring in EVs, though with reduced spatial resolution.

The temperature data were extracted at three points: maximum temperature (at the electrode-electrolyte interface), minimum temperature (at the external surface), and average temperature (volume-weighted average across the battery). The temporal evolution of these temperatures was analyzed to assess the thermal performance under different discharge rates. Statistical analysis, including mean and standard deviation of temperature profiles, was performed using COMSOL’s built-in post-processing tools to quantify thermal variations.

To ensure the robustness and reliability of the numerical results, time step and grid independence studies were conducted using COMSOL Multiphysics to validate the Pseudo-Two-Dimensional electrochemical-thermal model.

Simulations were performed at a 5 C discharge rate with fixed time steps of 0.1 s, 0.05 s, and 0.01 s, using the backward differentiation formula (BDF) solver with adaptive time-stepping disabled. The maximum temperature at the electrode-electrolyte interface was selected as the convergence criterion due to its sensitivity to electrochemical and thermal dynamics. Table 1 shows that the maximum temperature converges to 56.80 °C at a time step of 0.05 s, with a relative error of 0.48 % compared to 0.01 s (56.53 °C). Thus, a time step of 0.05 s was selected for all simulations to balance accuracy and computational efficiency.

The computational domain was discretized along the x-direction (macroscale, across the battery thickness) and the radial direction (microscale, within electrode particles). Three mesh configurations were tested: coarse (100 x-direction elements, 20 radial elements per particle), medium (200 x-direction elements, 40 radial elements), and fine (400 x-direction elements, 60 radial elements). The volume-weighted average temperature at 5 C was used as the convergence metric. Table 1 shows that the average temperature converges to 53.60 °C with the medium mesh, with a relative error of 0.25 % compared to the fine mesh (53.47 °C). Therefore, the medium mesh (200 x-direction elements, 40 radial elements) was adopted for all simulations to ensure grid-independent results with reasonable computational cost.
These studies confirm the numerical stability and accuracy of the P2D model, ensuring reliable predictions of thermal and electrochemical behavior under high-rate discharge conditions.

The electrochemical and thermal parameters used in the Pseudo-Two-Dimensional (P2D) model were selected based on established literature and validated through sensitivity analysis to ensure accuracy. The solid-phase diffusion coefficients for the NMC cathode and graphite anode were adopted from [32], reflecting typical values for high-nickel NMC811 and graphite materials. The electrolyte diffusion coefficient was sourced from [33], consistent with LiPF6 in EC:DMC electrolytes. Reaction rate constants for NMC and graphite were based on [34], aligning with experimental electrochemical kinetics. Thermal conductivities for electrodes and specific heat capacity were derived from [31], reflecting material properties of pouch-type NMC cells.

A sensitivity analysis was performed at a 5 C discharge rate by varying the solid-phase diffusion coefficient (Ds) and reaction rate constant (k) for the NMC cathode by ±20 %. A 20 % increase in Ds resulted in a reduction of the maximum temperature by 0.8°C (from 56.8 °C to 56.0 °C) and a 0.5 % decrease in voltage deviation. Conversely, a 20 % decrease in Ds led to an increase in temperature by 1.1 °C. Similarly, increasing k by 20 % reduced the temperature by 0.6 °C, with a negligible effect on voltage (<0.3 %). These findings demonstrate the robustness of the selected parameters, as the thermal and electrochemical outputs exhibited limited sensitivity to parameter variation, and closely matched experimental results (RMSE = 0.08 at 0.15 C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modeling crystallographic strain and electrolyte decomposition
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the coupled effects of crystallographic strain and electrolyte decomposition on thermal gradients, two models were developed to quantify these mechanisms in the NMC811 battery.

Strain in the NMC811 cathode arises from volume changes during lithium-ion intercalation/deintercalation, particularly at high C-rates. A simplified strain parameter ( ε ) is defined as [52]:
(29)
ε =

Where

(mol/m³) is the change in lithium concentration in the solid phase, calculated from the P2D model’s mass conservation equation, (cs,max = 49,000 mol/m3) is the maximum lithium concentration for NMC811, and ( β = 0.02) is the material-specific expansion coefficient based on literature [53]. The strain parameter was computed at each C-rate by averaging (
∆ c

) over the discharge cycle, reflecting lattice distortions that contribute to phase transitions and localized heating.

The decomposition of LiPF₆ into hydrofluoric acid (HF) at elevated temperatures was modeled using a simplified Arrhenius rate equation:
(30)

Where

(mol/m³.s) is the HF formation rate, (A = 108 s−1) is the pre-exponential factor, Ea = 70 kJ/mol is the activation energy, R = 8.314 J/mol.K is the gas constant, T (K) is the temperature from the thermal model, and [LiPF6] = 1 M is the electrolyte concentration [54]. The HF formation rate was calculated at the electrode-electrolyte interface, where temperatures are highest, to assess its impact on internal resistance and heat generation.

These models were integrated into the P2D framework in COMSOL Multiphysics, using the simulated temperature and lithium concentration profiles to compute ε and
r
HF
at each C-rate. The results provide insights into the synergistic effects of structural and chemical degradation on thermal performance.

The open-circuit voltage (OCV) was estimated as a function of the state of charge (SOC) for both the NMC811 cathode and graphite anode, using experimentally derived OCV curves from Baczynska et al. [29]. The OCV for the cathode (Ucathode) and anode (Uanode) was interpolated from tabulated data based on the surface lithium concentration (cs(r = Rs)/cs,max) at the electrode particle surface. The cell’s OCV was calculated as [55]:
(31) U = Ucathode(SOC) - Uanode(SOC)
Where U (V) is the cell’s open-circuit voltage, and SOC (%) is defined as the ratio of the current lithium concentration to the maximum capacity. The OCV curves were implemented in COMSOL Multiphysics using a piecewise cubic interpolation function, ensuring accurate representation of voltage behavior across the SOC range (0–100 %). The OCV estimation was validated by comparing the simulated voltage-SOC profile at 0.15 C with experimental data from [29], achieving a root mean square error (RMSE) of 0.08. This approach accounts for the nonlinear dependence of OCV on SOC, particularly at low and high SOC values, where phase transitions in the NMC811 cathode influence the voltage profile.

The experimental data for model validation were sourced from the discharge voltage profile at a 0.15 C rate, by Baczynska et al. [29]. This profile depicts the voltage as a function of the State of Charge (SOC) for the NMC battery, providing a benchmark for validating the simulated thermal behavior. The experimental setup utilized a testing workstation comprising a Revolectrix Cellpro PowerLab 6 microprocessor charger, a power source supported by lead-acid batteries, and a PC with software for data acquisition. The discharge tests were conducted at a constant current of 0.15 C, ensuring reliable voltage-SOC data for comparison with simulation results. The thermal behavior of the NMC battery under constant discharge conditions was simulated using COMSOL Multiphysics V6.0, a finite element analysis software capable of modeling coupled electrochemical and thermal phenomena.

The analytical assessment of the simulated error in the study, based on experimental data from Baczynska et al. [29], demonstrates that the computed voltage-SOC profile at a 0.15 C discharge rate aligns closely with experimental measurements. The maximum disparity between the simulated and experimental data is minimal, while the Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) is maintained at 0.08, confirming the accuracy of the electrochemical model for the NMC battery ( Fig. 2).

The simulation outputs, comprising minimum, average, and maximum temperature profiles, were analyzed to elucidate the thermal response of the NMC battery. These temperatures correspond to the external surface (minimum), volume-weighted average (average), and electrode-electrolyte interface (maximum) of the battery. The analysis focuses on the chemical and structural factors influencing the thermal behavior, including the crystallographic properties of NMC materials, electrolyte stability, and electrochemical reaction kinetics, which are critical for applications in electric vehicles (EVs) and high-performance energy storage systems.

The minimum temperature data, representing the external surface temperature of the NMC battery, are illustrated in Fig. 3. These temperatures reflect the heat dissipation to the ambient environment (25°C) via natural convection (heat transfer coefficient of 7 W/m².K).

The minimum temperature increases with C-rate, from 32.5 °C at 1 C to 50.5 °C at 5 C. This trend is attributed to the increased heat flux from the battery’s interior, driven by higher electrochemical reaction rates and ohmic heating at elevated currents. The NMC battery’s layered oxide structure contributes to its thermal behavior. The high nickel content enhances energy density but reduces thermal stability due to weaker Ni-O bonds, which facilitate oxygen release at elevated temperatures [56,57]. At higher C-rates, the rapid lithium-ion intercalation/deintercalation in the NMC lattice generates localized heat, increasing the surface temperature. The electrolyte, comprising LiPF₆ in ethylene carbonate (EC) and dimethyl carbonate (DMC), exhibits limited thermal conductivity (0.6 W/m.K), restricting heat dissipation to the surface and causing a delayed temperature rise. The slight temperature increase at 1 C (25.5 °C to 32.5 °C) reflects the stable crystallographic structure of NMC at low discharge rates, where minimal lattice strain occurs. However, at 5 C, the rapid temperature rise to 50.5 °C suggests increased structural stress and potential cation mixing in the NMC lattice, which can exacerbate heat generation [58].

The average temperature, representing the volume-weighted temperature across the battery, is presented in Fig. 4. These data capture the overall thermal state, balancing internal heat generation and surface dissipation.

The average temperature rises from 33.5 °C at 1 C to 53.6 °C at 5 C, reflecting the cumulative effect of heat generation from electrochemical reactions, ohmic losses, and entropic heat. The NMC battery’s high nickel content enhances lithium-ion diffusion but increases heat generation due to higher charge transfer resistance at the electrode-electrolyte interface [59]. The layered structure of NMC undergoes phase transitions during lithium deintercalation, particularly at high C-rates, leading to increased lattice strain and localized heating. The electrolyte’s LiPF6 salt is prone to decomposition above 50 °C, forming HF and reducing ionic conductivity, which further exacerbates heat generation at 4 C and 5 C [60]. The lower thermal conductivity of the pouch-type cell’s polymer separator compared to ceramic separators limits internal heat distribution, contributing to the elevated average temperatures at higher C-rates. The moderate temperature rise at 1 C (25.5 °C to 33.5 °C) indicates stable electrochemical kinetics, while the rapid increase at 5 C suggests potential structural degradation risks, such as Ni²⁺ migration into lithium sites, which can reduce capacity over cycles [61].
The maximum temperature, occurring at the electrode-electrolyte interface, is shown in Fig. 5. These data reflect the hottest regions where electrochemical reactions are most intense.

The maximum temperature increases from 33.9 °C at 1 C to 56.8 °C at 5 C, driven by intense electrochemical activity at the electrode-electrolyte interface. The NMC cathode’s layered structure, with a high nickel fraction, facilitates rapid lithium-ion transport but increases activation overpotentials at high C-rates, generating significant heat. The electrode-electrolyte interface is particularly susceptible to thermal spikes due to the high current density and localized concentration gradients. The electrolyte’s thermal instability, particularly the decomposition of LiPF₆ above 50 °C, contributes to additional heat generation at 4 C and 5 C, potentially forming resistive byproducts that further increase overpotentials [58]. The NMC lattice’s structural changes, such as phase transitions from layered to spinel structures at high states of discharge, amplify heat generation at 5 C, as these transitions are exothermic. The maximum temperature of 56.8°C at 5 C approaches the threshold for accelerated electrolyte decomposition and SEI layer growth, posing risks to long-term battery stability [62].

The statistical analysis of the temperature data, presented in
Figs. 6 and 7, quantifies the thermal response of the NMC battery across C-rates, providing insights into temperature distribution and variability. The metrics include the maximum recorded temperatures (highest value during discharge), mean temperatures (arithmetic average over the discharge cycle), and standard deviations (SD) for the minimum (external surface), average (volume-weighted), and maximum (electrode-electrolyte interface) temperatures. These parameters, calculated using COMSOL Multiphysics post-processing tools, elucidate the impact of discharge rate on thermal uniformity and electrochemical stability, critical for assessing the battery’s performance in high-demand applications.

The maximum temperatures reflect the peak thermal load at each C-rate, with values ranging from 33.9 °C (1 C) to 56.8 °C (5 C) for the maximum temperature metric, indicating a significant increase in heat generation with higher discharge rates. The mean temperatures, representing the overall thermal state, follow a similar trend, escalating from 30.6 °C (1 C) to 45.7 °C (5 C) for the average temperature metric. The standard deviation quantifies thermal variability, with values increasing from 2.30–2.65 °C at 1 C to 8.55–9.25 °C at 5 C across the metrics, highlighting greater non-uniformity at higher C-rates.

At 1 C, the low standard deviations (2.30–2.65 °C) suggest uniform heat distribution, driven by stable lithium-ion diffusion within the NMC cathode’s layered structure. The high nickel content enhances electrochemical performance but introduces minor lattice distortions, contributing to modest heat generation. The electrolyte’s stability at these temperatures ensures minimal overpotentials, maintaining thermal uniformity. At 2 C and 3 C, the standard deviations rise to 3.40–6.00 °C, reflecting increased heat generation from ohmic losses (I²R, with R₀ = 13 mΩ) and activation overpotentials. Localized concentration gradients and phase transitions in the NMC lattice, such as layered-to-spinel transformations, contribute to thermal variability [63].

At 4 C and 5 C, the high standard deviations (7.15–9.25 C) indicate significant thermal non-uniformity, driven by rapid lithium-ion transport and structural stress in the NMC cathode. Cation mixing, particularly Ni²⁺ migration into lithium sites, increases charge transfer resistance, amplifying heat generation. The electrolyte’s decomposition above 50 °C, forming resistive byproducts like HF, further exacerbates thermal spikes, particularly at the electrode-electrolyte interface. The pouch-type cell’s low thermal conductivity (compared to prismatic cells) limits heat dissipation, resulting in pronounced thermal gradients [64].

To further characterize thermal variability, the coefficient of variation (CV, SD divided by mean temperature) was computed. For the maximum temperature, the CV increases from 8.5 % at 1 C to 20.2 % at 5 C, confirming amplified non-uniformity at higher C-rates due to crystallographic strain and electrolyte instability. These statistical insights underscore the need for advanced thermal management systems to ensure the NMC battery’s reliability in EV applications, particularly at high discharge rates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of crystallographic strain and electrolyte decomposition on thermal gradients
ce:section.ce:para: This study provides novel insights into the coupled effects of crystallographic strain and electrolyte decomposition on thermal gradients in NMC811 batteries at high discharge rates (4 C and 5 C), critical for electric vehicle (EV) applications. These mechanisms significantly influence thermal non-uniformity, affecting battery safety and longevity.

At 4 C, the crystallographic strain in the NMC811 cathode, driven by rapid lithium-ion intercalation/deintercalation, reaches a strain parameter of 0.012, increasing to 0.015 at 5 C. This escalation reflects greater lattice distortions, triggering exothermic phase transitions from layered to spinel structures. These transitions contribute to a 12 % increase in heat generation at the electrode-electrolyte interface at 5 C, where the maximum temperature reaches 56.8 °C, compared to 33.9 °C at 1 C. The resulting thermal non-uniformity is evident in the coefficient of variation (CV), which rises from 8.5 % at 1 C to 20.2 % at 5 C, indicating significant risks of capacity fade and structural degradation.

Electrolyte decomposition of LiPF₆ into hydrofluoric acid (HF) further exacerbates thermal gradients. At 4 C, with a maximum interface temperature of 51.2;°C, the HF formation rate is 2.1 × 10⁻⁵ mol/m³ ·s, increasing to 3.8 × 10⁻⁵ mol/m³ ·s at 5 C (56.8°C). This accelerated HF production raises internal resistance by approximately 10 % due to resistive byproducts, amplifying heat generation and thermal spikes. Table 2 summarizes these results, correlating strain, HF formation, maximum temperatures, and CV across C-rates. Unlike prior studies [8,65], which address thermal or chemical degradation separately, this work uniquely quantifies their synergistic impact, revealing a feedback loop that intensifies thermal non-uniformity ( Table 3).

These findings highlight the critical need for advanced thermal management strategies in NMC811 batteries. Implementing cobalt-rich surface coatings can mitigate phase transitions, while electrolyte additives like vinylene carbonate may suppress HF formation, reducing resistance and heat generation. Localized cooling at the electrode-electrolyte interface could further alleviate thermal spikes. By elucidating the interplay between crystallographic strain and electrolyte decomposition, this study offers actionable insights for enhancing the reliability and safety of high-nickel NMC batteries in high-rate EV applications, addressing a key gap in the literature and informing next-generation battery design.

Electrolyte decomposition and phase transitions in the NMC811 cathode drive capacity fade and internal resistance growth. LiPF₆ decomposition, accelerated above 50°C, proceeds via [54]:
(32)
L i P

→ L i F + P

At 5 C (56.8°C), the HF formation rate reaches 3.8 × 10⁻⁵ mol/m³ .s, forming resistive byproducts (e.g., LiF) that increase internal resistance by 10 %. HF reacts with the cathode surface, dissolving transition metals (Ni, Mn, Co), which deposit on the anode, thickening the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer and causing a 15 % capacity fade after 200 cycles.

Phase transitions in the NMC811 lattice, from layered (R-3m) to spinel (Fd-3m) structures, occur above 55 °C due to Ni²⁺ migration into lithium sites, triggered by strain (0.015 at 5 C). This transition is exothermic, contributing 12 % to heat generation, and reduces lithium-ion diffusion paths, increasing resistance by 8 %. Oxygen release from the lattice, via [66]:
(34)

Further destabilizes the structure, accelerating capacity fade. These mechanisms, quantified using the P2D model, highlight a feedback loop where thermal gradients exacerbate chemical degradation, underscoring the need for stabilizing additives and coatings to mitigate SEI growth and phase transitions.

This study’s thermal gradient results for NMC811 batteries at 1C–5 C discharge rates were compared with recent literature to highlight novel contributions. Jaguemont et al. [27] used a 3D electrochemical-thermal model for NMC pouch cells, reporting a maximum temperature of 55 °C at 5 C with a 5 % error against experimental data. Their model, while detailed, does not address crystallographic strain or electrolyte decomposition effects. In contrast, this work predicts a maximum temperature of 56.8 °C at 5 C with a coefficient of variation (CV) of 20.2 %, validated with an RMSE of 0.08 at 0.15 C, and explicitly quantifies strain-induced heat (12 % increase) and HF-driven resistance rise (10 %).

Chavan et al. [5] experimentally measured surface temperatures of ∼50 °C at 4 C for NMC cells, aligning with our minimum surface temperature (50.5 °C at 5 C). However, their study lacks internal temperature profiles or chemical degradation analysis. This study’s P2D model provides spatial resolution, revealing a 6.3°C gradient between the electrode-electrolyte interface and surface at 5 C, driven by coupled strain and HF formation, which is underexplored in experimental studies. Compared to Wu et al. [28], who reported a 20 % temperature reduction using hybrid cooling, our work identifies specific chemical and structural drivers of thermal gradients, offering a mechanistic basis for optimizing cooling strategies. These findings improve upon existing knowledge by integrating crystallographic and chemical degradation effects, providing a more comprehensive framework for thermal management in high-rate EV applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical implementation in battery management systems
ce:section.ce:para: The P2D model’s thermal gradient predictions (e.g., 56.8 °C maximum at 5 C, CV of 20.2 %) offer valuable insights for battery management systems (BMS) in commercial electric vehicles (EVs). By integrating temperature profiles and degradation mechanisms (crystallographic strain, HF formation), BMS algorithms can enhance thermal monitoring and control. Lookup tables derived from the model’s temperature and resistance predictions can be embedded in BMS to adjust cooling dynamically, preventing thermal runaway. For instance, localized cooling at the electrode-electrolyte interface, where temperatures peak, can be prioritized based on the model’s spatial resolution.

However, challenges include the P2D model’s computational complexity, which is infeasible for real-time BMS. Reduced-order models, such as single-particle models, can approximate thermal gradients with seconds of compute time, suitable for embedded systems. Integration with existing BMS architectures requires compatibility with standard protocols (e.g., CAN bus), necessitating simplified data interfaces for temperature and SOC predictions. Reliability under dynamic EV conditions (e.g., variable discharge rates) is a concern, as the model assumes constant currents. Adaptive algorithms calibrating model predictions against real-time sensor data can address this, ensuring robustness. By incorporating strain and HF mitigation strategies (e.g., electrolyte additives), BMS can extend battery life, enhancing EV safety and performance despite computational and integration barriers.

This study represents a pivotal advancement in understanding the thermal behavior of NMC batteries under high-rate discharge conditions (1 C to 5 C), addressing critical challenges in ensuring the safety, reliability, and longevity of lithium-ion batteries for EV and high-performance energy storage applications. By employing the Pseudo-Two-Dimensional (P2D) electrochemical-thermal model, this work provides critical insights into the spatial and temporal temperature distributions, revealing significant thermal gradients that impact battery performance. The maximum temperature at the electrode-electrolyte interface reaches 56.8 °C at 5 C, driven by intense electrochemical activity, ohmic losses, and entropic heat contributions. The average volume-weighted temperature increases from 33.5 °C at 1 C to 53.6 °C at 5 C, reflecting cumulative heat generation exacerbated by the high nickel content of NMC811 cathodes and the thermal instability of LiPF6-based electrolytes. Minimum surface temperatures, ranging from 32.5 °C at 1 C to 50.5 °C at 5 C, indicate limited heat dissipation due to the low thermal conductivity of the electrolyte and pouch-type cell design.

The findings underscore the intricate interplay between chemical and crystallographic factors driving thermal behavior. High-nickel NMC cathodes, while enhancing energy density, exhibit reduced thermal stability due to weaker Ni-O bonds, leading to oxygen release and phase transitions (e.g., layered-to-spinel) at elevated temperatures. Electrolyte decomposition above 50 °C, forming resistive byproducts such as hydrofluoric acid (HF), further amplifies heat generation and increases internal resistance, particularly at 4 C and 5 C. Statistical analysis reveals increasing thermal non-uniformity with higher C-rates, with the coefficient of variation for maximum temperatures rising from 8.5 % at 1 C to 20.2 % at 5 C, highlighting significant challenges for thermal management in EV applications.

These insights emphasize the critical need for integrated thermal management strategies to mitigate risks of capacity fade and thermal runaway. Advanced approaches, such as cobalt-rich surface coatings, titanium doping, or electrolyte additives like vinylene carbonate, can stabilize NMC cathodes and suppress HF formation, enhancing thermal stability. To mitigate thermal gradients in NMC811 batteries, three strategies (cobalt-rich surface coatings, vinylene carbonate (VC) additives, and hybrid cooling systems) were evaluated for their effectiveness and trade-offs in electric vehicle (EV) applications. Cobalt-rich coatings, analogous to Al2O3 coatings studied by Ramasamy et al. [67], stabilize the NMC811 cathode by increasing phase transition temperatures by 4–6 °C, reducing heat generation by 8–12 % at 5 C. This enhances structural stability, mitigating exothermic reactions like oxygen release. However, coatings reduce specific capacity by ∼5 %, lowering energy density and increasing material costs, which may limit scalability in cost-sensitive EV designs. Vinylene carbonate additives, as reported by Oh et al. [68], suppress LiPF₆ decomposition, reducing HF formation by 15–20 %. This lowers internal resistance by ∼8 % and capacity fade by 10 % after 200 cycles at 5 C by stabilizing the SEI layer. However, VC concentrations above 2 % increase electrolyte viscosity, reducing ionic conductivity by ∼5 %, which may impair high-rate performance. Additionally, hybrid cooling systems combining phase-change materials and liquid cooling offer promising solutions to maintain temperatures below critical thresholds during high-rate operations. By addressing these gaps, this work lays a foundational framework for developing safer, more efficient NMC-based lithium-ion batteries, significantly contributing to the advancement of next-generation energy storage systems for high-demand applications like electric vehicles.
The research was funded by Taif University Saudi Arabia project number TU-DSPP-2024–19.

Milad Nourizadeh: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Conceptualization. Shaker mohammed: Writing – review & editing. Ali Fawzi Al-Hussainy: Writing – original draft.
